summary: Beta-oxidation of fatty acids occurs in the mitochondria and produces acetyl-CoA, NADH, and FADH2.
Beta-oxidation occurs in the cytoplasm and produces glucose.
Beta-oxidation occurs in the peroxisomes and produces lactate.
HMG-CoA reductase is the rate-limiting enzyme of cholesterol synthesis and the target of statins.
HMG-CoA synthase is the rate-limiting enzyme of cholesterol synthesis.
Cholesterol synthesis occurs mainly in the kidneys, not the liver.
LDL deposits cholesterol in arterial walls (bad cholesterol), while HDL performs reverse cholesterol transport (good cholesterol).
HDL deposits cholesterol in arterial walls, while LDL removes it.
VLDL is the primary carrier of cholesterol to peripheral tissues.
The carnitine shuttle (CPT-1 is rate-limiting) transports long-chain fatty acids into the mitochondria for beta-oxidation.
Fatty acids enter mitochondria freely without any transport mechanism.
The carnitine shuttle transports glucose into the mitochondria.
Lipids are classified into simple (fats, waxes), compound (phospholipids, glycolipids), and derived (cholesterol, steroids) lipids.
Lipids are classified into saturated, unsaturated, and trans lipids only.
All lipids are classified as either fats or oils based on their melting point.
Ketone bodies are produced in the liver when acetyl-CoA accumulates from excessive beta-oxidation during fasting or uncontrolled diabetes.
Ketone bodies are produced in muscle tissue during exercise.
Ketone bodies are a normal product of glycolysis that increases after meals.
Essential fatty acids (linoleic and linolenic acid) cannot be synthesised by the body and must be obtained from the diet.
All fatty acids can be synthesised by the body from glucose.
Essential fatty acids are only needed during childhood growth.
